1: ki boshesh Mosheh laradet (for Moses delayed/was long in coming down) — boshesh (H954, from bosh, to be ashamed, to be long in coming) idiomatically means 'he took a long time.' The verb bosh also means 'to be put to shame'; there may be an ironic undertone — the people will experience profound shame by the end of this episode. Elohim asher yelchu lefaneinu (gods who will go before us) — whether grammatically plural-of-majesty or genuinely polytheistic, the narrative treats the golden calf as a categorical violation of the first two commandments (Exodus 20:3-4). The people are not asking for a different religion; they want a visible divine proxy — precisely what the invisible Sinai God refuses to be.
2: parqu nizme hazahav (tear off the gold rings) — Aaron's compliance with the demand is immediate; no resistance is recorded. Nizme hazahav (rings of gold) — the same gold earrings given as plunder from Egypt (Exodus 3:22; Exodus 11:2; Exodus 12:35-36). The material for the golden calf is the spoils of the exodus itself: what was God's provision for the tabernacle is diverted to idolatry.
3: vayitparqu kol ha'am (all the people tore off) — collective, voluntary compliance. The gold that was to furnish God's tabernacle now funds Israel's apostasy. The juxtaposition with Exodus 35:21-22 (when the people bring freewill offerings for the tabernacle) is deliberate: the same people, the same material, two radically different dispositions.
4: vayitsar oto bacharet (he fashioned it with an engraving tool) — charet (H2747, stylus/engraving tool) appears only here in the OT. Aaron uses an artisan's tool — exactly the kind of tool Bezalel and Oholiab will use — to shape the calf from molten gold. Egel massekhah (a cast calf/idol) — massekha (H4541, poured/cast image) from nasakh (to pour, to cast metal). The contrast with Bezalel's Spirit-filled craftsmanship (Exodus 31) is stark: the same skill, the same material, but the opposite commission. Ele eloheicha Yisrael asher he'elucha me'eretz Mitzrayim (These are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up from Egypt) — this formula echoes 1 Kings 12:28 (Jeroboam's golden calves), confirming that Exodus 32 is the canonical prototype for all subsequent calf-worship in the northern kingdom.
5: vayar Aharon vayiven mizbeach lefanav (Aaron saw and built an altar before it) — Aaron's response to the calf is construction of an altar, deepening the sin. Vayiqra Aharon vayomer chag laYHWH machar (and Aaron called out and said, 'a feast to the LORD tomorrow') — Aaron uses the covenant name YHWH for what is now calf-worship. This is not pure paganism (abandoning YHWH) but syncretic appropriation of YHWH's name for an idolatrous rite. The theological danger of syncretism is precisely this: the name is preserved while the nature is violated.
6: vayashkimu mimmacharrat vayyaalu olot (they rose early the next morning and offered burnt offerings) — the religious form is correct (olot and shelamim); the object is wrong. Vayeshev ha'am le'echol veshato vayaqumu lesachaq (the people sat down to eat and drink, and rose to revel) — sachaq (H6711, to laugh, to play, to revel) — the same root used in Genesis 26:8 for intimate play between Isaac and Rebekah. Paul cites this verse in 1 Corinthians 10:7, warning against idolatry; his quotation of lehatzchiq (to play) in the LXX form paizein suggests he reads the revelry as including sexual immorality alongside idolatry.
7: shichet amcha asher he'elita me'eretz Mitzrayim (your people, whom you brought out of the land of Egypt, have corrupted themselves) — shichet (H7843, to destroy/corrupt/ruin) is the same verb applied to Noah's generation (Genesis 6:12: ki hishchit kol basar). God's dissociation is immediate: 'your people' (amcha) instead of 'my people' — a shift in possessive pronoun that signals the covenant strain. The formula mirrors exactly the pre-flood judgment language; the golden calf episode is Israel's equivalent of the generation of Noah's corruption.
8: saru meheir min haderech (they turned aside quickly from the way) — sar (H5493, to turn aside, to deviate) from haderech (the way) — the Torah repeatedly uses 'the way' as the path of covenant obedience (Deuteronomy 5:33; Exodus 9:12). Turning aside quickly (meheir) highlights that the covenant violation followed the covenant ratification by days. Vayishtochu lo vayizbechu lo (and bowed down to it and sacrificed to it) — the two forbidden acts of the second commandment.
9: am qesheh oref hu (it is a stiff-necked people) — qasheh oref (H7186+H6203, stiff/hardened neck) — the neck/back-of-neck metaphor for stubbornness comes from plowing: an ox that refuses to yield to the yoke stiffens its neck. This phrase becomes the defining characterization of Israel throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 9:6,13; Exodus 10:16) and in the prophets; Stephen uses it in Acts 7:51 when indicting the Sanhedrin.
10: ve'ata hanichah li veyichar-api vahem (now leave me alone, that my anger may burn against them) — chara (H2734, to burn/glow with anger) appears in the divine emotional vocabulary. Va'e'eseh otcha legoy gadol (and I will make of you a great nation) — the offer to restart the covenant through Moses alone reveals both the seriousness of Israel's failure and the genuineness of Moses' subsequent intercession: Moses could have accepted.
11: vayechal Mosheh et penei YHWH Elohav (Moses implored/appeased the face of the LORD his God) — chalah (H2470, to be weak/sick; in piel: to entreat, to implore) — the idiom describes desperate, costly prayer. Penei YHWH (the face of the LORD) — to implore God's face is to seek direct audience. Moses identifies himself with the people he could have been separated from: 'the LORD his God' rather than 'their God.'
12: lama yomeru Mitzrayim lemor (why should Egypt say) — Moses' first argument is apologetic: what will the nations conclude? The vindication of God's name among the nations requires Israel's survival. Shub mecharon appeka vehinnachem al hara'ah le'ammekha (turn from your burning anger and relent of the evil against your people) — Moses commands God to relent using the same vocabulary God uses for prophetic oracles. Nachem (H5162, to be moved to compassion, to relent) is a theological claim that prayer genuinely affects divine action.
13: zachor leAvraham leYitschaq uleYisrael avadecha (remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, your servants) — Moses' second argument is covenantal memory. Zachor (H2142, to remember, to be mindful of) — when directed at God, it is not informing God of forgotten facts but calling on God to act in accordance with prior commitments. Asher nishba'ta lahem bach (to whom you swore by yourself) — God swore by himself (Genesis 22:16-17) because there was nothing greater by which to swear; Moses uses the irreversibility of a divine self-oath as his ultimate argument.
14: vayinnachem YHWH al hara'ah asher dibber la'asot le'ammo (the LORD relented from the evil/disaster he had spoken of bringing on his people) — vayinnachem (H5162, nifal: to be moved/to relent) — the same verb used in Genesis 6:6 when God 'regretted' making humanity. Here it describes a genuine divine response to intercession. The verb is not a change in God's eternal decree but a change in immediate temporal action — the clearest biblical demonstration that intercessory prayer accomplishes something real within the divine economy.
15: luchot ha'edut (tablets of the testimony/covenant) — as Moses descends, he carries the objects described in Exodus 31:18: the two stone tablets written by God. Mikhtav Elohim hu (it is God's writing) — the writing on the tablets is divine autograph, the most direct divine communication in the Torah outside of theophanic speech. Their impending destruction (v19) is thus not merely the loss of stone; it is the symbolic fracture of the covenant itself.
16: charut al haluchot (engraved/incised on the tablets) — charut (H2801, incised/engraved) — the law is cut into stone, permanent. The word is homophonous with cherut (freedom/liberty, H2668), prompting the classic rabbinic wordplay: 'read not charut (engraved) but cherut (freedom) — for no one is free except one who occupies himself with Torah' (m. Avot Exodus 6:2). The play captures the Torah's own self-understanding that covenant obligation and genuine freedom are not opposites.
17: vayishma Yehoshua et qol ha'am bereaoto (Joshua heard the sound of the people in their reveling) — Joshua interprets the noise as the sound of war (qol milchama); Moses corrects him: it is singing (qol anot anochi shomea). The military ear hears battle; the prophetic ear hears apostasy. Moses' correction is diagnostic — the noise is recognizable as neither triumph nor lamentation but the uninhibited sound of religious festivity gone wrong.
18: qol anot anochi shomea (the sound of singing I hear) — anot (H6030, to answer/respond in song) — Moses identifies it specifically as antiphonal song, the musical form of the victory odes (Exod 15) now appropriated for calf-worship. The same musical form that celebrated the exodus from Egypt is now used to worship the substitute god fashioned from Egypt's gold.
19: vayashlach miyyadav et haluchot vayshaber otam tachat hahar (he threw the tablets from his hands and shattered them at the foot of the mountain) — Moses' shattering of the tablets is not a loss of temper but a covenantal act: the covenant documents are destroyed because the covenant has been violated. The tablets tachat hahar (at the foot of the mountain) — the precise site of the covenant ratification (Exodus 24:4-8) — underscores that the broken covenant is broken at the place it was made. Later Jewish interpretation reads Moses' act as legally correct, analogous to a divorce document (Deuteronomy 24:1): the covenant is dissolved before its terms are formally delivered.
20: vayiqach et ha'egel asher asu vayisrof ba'esh vayitchan ad asher-dak (he took the calf they had made, burned it with fire, and ground it to powder) — a three-stage destruction: burn, grind to dust (dak, H1851, fine/thin — same word as the incense in Exodus 30:36), scatter on water, force the people to drink. The enforced drinking of the gold-water is both degradation (you consume your own idolatry) and possibly a trial-by-ordeal parallel to the bitter water of Numbers 5:17-24 (the sotah ritual).
21: meh asah lecha ha'am hazeh ki-heveta alav chata gedolah (what did this people do to you that you brought so great a sin upon them?) — Moses' question to Aaron is prosecutorial. Chata gedolah (H2399+H1419, great sin) is the gravity-term for calf-worship that will echo through Israel's history: 1 Kings 12:30 uses the identical phrase for Jeroboam's calves. Aaron's answer (vv22-24) is a masterpiece of deflection: he blames the people, misrepresents the process (claiming the calf emerged spontaneously from the fire), and does not once use the first person for the act of fashioning.
22: al yichar af adoni (let not the anger of my lord burn) — Aaron addresses Moses as adoni (my lord), the deference of a subordinate. He then shifts blame entirely: ki vara hu ha'am hazeh (for this people is set on evil/wickedness) — ra (H7451, evil/wickedness) characterizes the people, not himself. The strategy of blame-shifting echoes Adam (Genesis 3:12) and anticipates a recurring pattern in biblical leadership failure.
23: vayomeru li asah lanu elohim asher yelchu lefaneinu (they said to me, make us gods who will go before us) — Aaron quotes back the people's demand verbatim (cf. v1), establishing the source of the pressure. The defense is accurate but self-exculpatory: he did receive the demand, but he complied without resistance. There is no recorded protest from Aaron comparable to Moses' intercession in vv11-13.
24: va'omar lahem lemi zahav hitparaqу (I said to them, whoever has gold, take it off) — Aaron's version collapses the process: he collected the gold and threw it into the fire, and vayetze ha'egel hazeh (and this calf came out). The passive construction ('this calf came out') is a studied denial of personal agency; in v4 the narrator clearly states vayitsar oto bacharet (he fashioned it with a tool). Aaron's testimony is self-contradictory within the same chapter — a textual marker of evasion.
25: ki fara hu (for it was unrestrained/running wild) — para (H6544, to lead/let go free, to let run wild) — the root is used for uncovering the head in disgrace (Leviticus 10:6; Exodus 13:45) and for letting hair grow wild (Numbers 5:18). Here it describes the moral-social breakdown of the camp. Ki feraa Aharon lishmtsa bequmeihem (for Aaron had let them run wild — to their shame among their adversaries) — Aaron's failure of leadership is explicitly named: he let them go, and the result is shimtsah (H8103, shame/mockery/infamy) — disgrace that invites enemies to mock.
26: vayya'amod Mosheh beshaar hamachaneh vayomer mi laYHWH eilai (Moses stood at the gate of the camp and said, whoever is for the LORD, come to me) — the gate position is judicial: Moses stands where verdicts are announced and cases heard. The call is a loyalty test — a forced division of the camp. Mi laYHWH eilai (whoever is for the LORD, to me) — the rallying cry becomes the Levites' defining moment of covenant fidelity.
27: shuvu veshabu mishaar leshaar bamachaneh vehirgu ish et achiv (return and go from gate to gate through the camp, and each man kill his brother) — the Levites execute the idolaters without regard for family ties. Ish et achiv ve'ish et reeihu ve'ish et qerovo (each man his brother, each man his companion, each man his neighbor) — the three-fold expansion covers every conceivable relationship: the killing cannot be softened by personal loyalty. This motif of covenant faithfulness over family reappears in Deuteronomy 33:9 and in Jesus's saying about bringing not peace but a sword (Matthew 10:34-37).
28: vayaaseh venei Levi kidvar Mosheh (the sons of Levi did according to the word of Moses) — faithful execution. Vayipol min ha'am bayom hahu keshloshlet alafim ish (about three thousand men fell that day) — the number marks the day as catastrophic but bounded: three thousand out of approximately 600,000 men. The same number, inverted, marks Pentecost: three thousand added to the church (Acts 2:41) — a pattern read as the reversal of Sinai's death by the Spirit's life-giving.
29: millu yedekhem hayom laYHWH (fill your hands today for the LORD) — milu yadekhem (H4390+H3027, fill your hands) is the technical installation formula for priestly ordination (Exodus 28:41; Leviticus 8:33). Here it is applied to the Levites in the context of killing rather than sacrifice — their 'ordination' to service was the day they chose covenant over family. Ki ish beveno uvachiv (each man at the cost of his son and his brother) — their ordination cost them human relationship; Deuteronomy reads this as their credential for priestly blessing (Deuteronomy 33:9-10).
30: veata e'eleh el YHWH ulai akappera beaad chatatkhem (now I will go up to the LORD; perhaps I will make atonement for your sin) — kaphar (H3722, to make atonement) — Moses frames his intercessory ascent using the priestly language of atonement. Ulai (perhaps) is striking: even Moses does not presume on the outcome of intercession for covenant-breaking apostasy. The verb kaphar here is used non-sacrificially; Moses' very person, offered in prayer, is the atoning medium.
31: ana chata ha'am hazeh chataah gedolah (alas, this people has sinned a great sin) — Moses begins his intercession with direct confession on behalf of the people, not minimization. Vayaasu lahem elohei zahav (they have made themselves gods of gold) — the confession names the specific sin. Priestly atonement in the Levitical system requires specification of the sin; Moses' intercession follows the same logic.
32: veatta im tissa chataam (and now, if you will forgive their sin) — the conditional prayer leaves the sentence incomplete — the apodosis ('then forgive them') is swallowed by the passion of what follows. Ve'im ayin mecheni na misiphrecha asher katavta (and if not, blot me out of your book that you have written) — Moses offers himself as a substitute: blot me, not them. Machah (H4229, to wipe/erase/blot out) — the most radical form of intercessory self-sacrifice in the OT. Paul echoes it in Romans 9:3 ('I could wish myself accursed'). The 'book' is the divine register of the living (Psalms 69:28; Daniel 12:1; Revelation 3:5; Exodus 20:12).
33: mi asher chata li emchenu misipri (whoever has sinned against me I will blot out of my book) — God declines Moses' substitutionary offer. Each person is accountable for their own sin; the divine book-keeping preserves individual responsibility. This is not a rejection of intercession but a refusal of substitution: Moses' life cannot ransom others' apostasy. Lekh nacha et ha'am (go, lead the people) — the commission continues despite the sin.
34: hineh malaachi yelech lefanecha (my angel will go before you) — the transition from direct divine presence to angelic mediation marks a fractured intimacy. The full-presence theophany (Exodus 13:21-22; Exodus 14:19-20) is replaced by an angelic representative. This mediatory distance becomes the subject of Moses' subsequent intercession in Exodus 33:12-17, where he presses for God's own presence, not merely his angel. Uveyom poqudi ufaqadti aleihem chataam (but in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them) — a delayed reckoning: God does not fully execute judgment now but reserves a future accounting.
35: vayiggof YHWH et ha'am (the LORD struck the people) — nagaf (H5062, to strike/afflict/plague) — the verb used for the plagues upon Egypt (Exodus 9:14) is now used for a plague upon Israel. The irony completes a terrible symmetry: the plagues came upon Egypt for Pharaoh's stiff-necked refusal to let Israel go; a plague comes upon Israel for their stiff-necked return to Egypt's religious patterns. The gold of Egypt, the form of Egypt's animal cults, the vocabulary of Egyptian oppression (shachat, bosh) — all return at Sinai as Israel's own judgment.
